narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your profile includes strong indicators of dedication, like planning to attend full-time, but also some academic metrics that raised concerns for the admissions model. The model predicts an 82.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also a 1, again signaling early underperformance compared to most students who rank higher. Your LSAT score of 28 further nudged the prediction toward failure, as it's notably lower than the average of about 37 seen in the dataset. These three academic markers stood out as the strongest drivers, each contributing positively to the failure risk because they deviated from patterns associated with bar exam success. In contrast, elements like your lower-middle family income bracket didn't sway the decision much, and your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar prediction, your grades and test score lag behind their stronger showings, tipping the scales against you despite your background. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male pursuing law—many overcome similar starts—but the model focuses on these predictors of bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your undergraduate record if possible through post-bac courses, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also help reframe your story for future applications. With targeted improvements, you can shift these key factors and improve your odds next time.
